data.body:
INVESTIGATION (why code-review findings score disproportionately low): root cause is that impact() measures PRODUCTION-INCIDENT severity, which is the wrong yardstick for a maintainability gate. impact = mean(max(prod_impact,debt_impact), blast_radius, likelihood, reversibility), scale floored at 0.33.

Evidence — attribute distributions, SAME model:
- PLAN-REVIEW would-block (n=921): prod_impact high=59%, blast module+=98%, likelihood high=78%, reversibility moderate/hard=84% => impact 0.78+.
- CODE-REVIEW (n=31): prod_impact none/low=96% (0% high), reversibility easy=93%, blast local/module, debt_impact never high => impact structurally capped ~0.25-0.60.

So plan-review findings are production-CONSEQUENCE-shaped (the model fits); code-review findings are MAINTENANCE/DETECTION-GAP-shaped (the model has no axis for them). Concrete perversities:
1. A silently-dead feature (untested wiring #10) is rated prod_impact=NONE — the model asks 'does it crash now', not 'does it fail to work / go undetected'.
2. reversibility INVERTS the signal: 'easy to reverse' lowers impact, but a missing test being easy-to-add-later is exactly why it's a latent landmine; the model rewards the wrong thing. 93% of code-review findings are 'easy' => everything pinned near the 0.33 floor on that axis.
3. debt_impact (the one axis meant for tech-debt) is capped — verifier never emits 'high', and it only counts via max(prod,debt).

Implication: code-review priority-blocking is not tunable by threshold (landmines & nits both compressed into 0.4-0.6, overlapping). Fixes: (a) a maintainability-appropriate impact model for the code_review gate — debt-primary, add a detection-gap/latent-regression axis, drop or invert reversibility for detection findings; and/or (b) block on a semantic CLASS (DET new-behavior-without-test) that sidesteps the broken scalar. This deepens the original idea: it's not just 'the mean dilutes' — the AXES are wrong for this gate + reversibility is inverted for detection findings.
